rationale: The citing paper uses Kivetz, Urminsky, and Zheng (2006) as core evidence for the goal-gradient account that progress toward a goal can increase persistence, especially in loyalty-program contexts. This citation appears in the conceptual development of the persistence pathway and is invoked again when motivating predictions about when progress cues should increase continued effort. So the cited work is part of the paper's theoretical setup, not just a passing literature-list mention, even though the new paper does not reuse its specific empirical design.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Kivetz et al. (2006) as a theoretical and empirical basis for the claim that perceived goal progress can accelerate persistent behavior. It then builds its PLRM framework on top of that foundation to explain heterogeneity in persistence versus licensing after subgoal success.
